item.title: The Iranian Prisons Where Bombs Are Threatening Dissidents and Americans
item.description: Airstrikes have damaged complexes used to hold political detainees, according to a Wall Street Journal visual investigation.

item.title: Opinion | The Tragedy of Robert Mueller
item.description: The Marine and FBI director’s long service was marred by the Russia collusion probe.

item.title: Opinion | Cuba Tries One More Big Con
item.description: Havana is inviting foreign investors again. Stephen Purvis knows how that goes.

item.title: Opinion | American Credibility Is on the Line in Iran
item.description: If Trump leaves the job unfinished, it will do to the U.S. what the Suez crisis did to Britain and France.

item.title: MAGA Allies in Europe Find Defending Trump Isn’t Always Easy
item.description: Parties that have embraced Trump are grappling with the fallout from U.S. policies and its impact on public opinion.

item.title: Jetliners and Missiles Are Sharing the Sky in the Middle East. Pilots Are Worried.
item.description: Plus, how the war is making U.S. stocks look a lot more attractive, and why now might be the best time to nab a cheap EV.

item.title: Opinion | The One Ally We Have in Dealing With Hormuz Is A Potent One
item.description: Israel is contributing significant strategic and operational wins that are easing the road to victory over the regime that declares “Death to America.”

item.title: How Police and Criminals Use Uganda’s Antigay Law to Find Blackmail Targets
item.description: Ever since a strict, new law took effect , the bars, restaurants and nightclubs of Kampala’s entertainment districts have become hunting grounds for those on the lookout for gay people to blackmail.

item.title: The New Weapons of Global Power Are Oil, Rare Earths and Microchips
item.description: Iran has wielded crude as a cudgel on a scale unseen in decades, marking a new phase in the 21st-century competition for dominance.

item.title: This Border Crossing Is One of Iran’s Last Lifelines to the Outside World
item.description: Iranians interviewed at one of the country’s few corridors to the outside world describe their escape to safety, as others return home and into the unknown.

item.title: Flights Are Still Taking Off as Missiles Rain Down in the Middle East
item.description: Dozens of flights have arrived in and departed Dubai within minutes of strikes, a WSJ analysis found.

item.title: Iran’s Houthi Allies Lie in Wait on Another Key Oil Route: the Red Sea
item.description: Tehran’s Houthi allies in Yemen have ramped up their threats after bottling up shipping for years.

item.title: Iran Brings Europe Into Range With Missiles Fired at Diego Garcia
item.description: Iran’s attack on the base 2,500 miles away broke its own stated limit on missiles and signaled a more-aggressive military posture for the regime

item.title: The Silicon Valley Salesman Accused of Helping China Get Nvidia’s Top Chips
item.description: A recently unsealed indictment naming Wally Liaw, co-founder of Super Micro Computer, puts the company at the center of the U.S.-China tech war.

item.title: Empires Have Battled Over the Strait of Hormuz for Centuries
item.description: Greeks, Ottomans and Portuguese all sought to control the Persian waterway, whose shores were once dubbed the Pirate Coast.

item.title: Where Is Mojtaba Khamenei? Iran Fills the Gap With AI and Voice-Overs
item.description: The new supreme leader has yet to be seen or heard from two weeks into the job. Some Iranians are calling him the “cardboard ayatollah.”

item.title: The Politician Defying France’s Unwritten Rule About the Far Right
item.description: Eric Ciotti was expelled from the conservative party over his alliance with Marine Le Pen. Now, he is vying to win control of one of the country’s largest cities.

item.title: Ukraine Is Suddenly on the Offensive, With Help From Elon Musk
item.description: Kyiv’s forces have notched their biggest domestic territorial gains in more than two years after Russia lost the use of Starlink.

item.title: Iran War Puts U.S. Diplomatic Missions in the Crosshairs
item.description: Drone and missile attacks as well as new terror threats have U.S. embassies worldwide on high alert.

item.title: U.S. Probes Colombian Leader for Alleged Use of Drug Money for Campaign
item.description: Gustavo Petro, who has clashed with President Trump, has been dogged by allegations that he benefited from drug money, which he denies.

item.title: Iran Believes It’s Winning—and Wants a Steep Price to End the War
item.description: Tehran sees an opportunity to control the Middle East’s energy as it bets on outlasting Trump’s will—a risky gamble.

item.title: The Quiet American: How Pope Leo Is Pushing Back Against Trump
item.description: Can the low-key pontiff from Chicago make a difference in an era of raw power politics?

item.title: Opinion | Iran Attacks the Gulf Arabs. Will They Fight Back?
item.description: These states can do more to help as Tehran blocks and targets their energy.

item.title: A Belligerent Iran Has the U.S. Rushing Missile and Drone Defenses to the Gulf
item.description: Arms shipments are on the way as regional allies run through stockpiles amid Tehran’s still-potent attacks.

item.title: Opinion | An Ally’s Guide to Understanding Trump
item.description: His tactic is to provoke negotiating partners into losing their cool. Canada falls for it every time.

item.title: Opinion | The U.S. Ammo Shortage Is Worse Than You Think
item.description: Congress and the Pentagon need to revitalize the U.S. defense industry base—and they need to act fast.

item.title: Opinion | One More Flaw in the 2015 Iran Nuclear Deal
item.description: The agreement not only failed to limit Iran’s strategic missile threat; it facilitated it.

item.title: From Four-Day Weeks to AC Bans, the World Is Scrambling to Save Energy
item.description: Governments are urging citizens to change their behavior to fend off the worst oil-and-gas crunch since the 1970s.

item.title: Giant South Pars Gas Field Is Iran’s Biggest Economic Safety Net
item.description: South Pars is the heart and lungs of an Iranian economy that relies heavily on natural gas to keep powering its homes, factories, cars and even its water pumps.

item.title: Brussels Moves to Lighten Emissions Load on Industry as Energy Prices Soar
item.description: The EU plans changes to its carbon-pricing program with the bloc’s industrial sector bracing for a spike in energy prices caused by the Middle East conflict.

item.title: South Korea, a ‘Model Ally,’ Confronts a Leaner U.S. Military Alliance
item.description: The security relationship that allies, particularly in Asia, have with the U.S. is evolving into an era where they may be called to do more with less.

item.title: Russia Central Bank Lowers Key Rate Despite Oil-Price Boost
item.description: The Bank of Russia reduced borrowing costs to 15% even as a surge in oil prices promises to boost an economy that had been faltering.

item.title: In Nearly 90 Truth Social Posts, Trump Narrates the War in Iran
item.description: Three weeks into the conflict, the president has used social media to conduct his own unique form of online diplomacy.

item.title: Asian Central Banks Shift to Sidelines as Mideast Conflict Drags On
item.description: As the Middle East conflict sends energy prices soaring, markets are watching to see how policymakers will respond this time around.

item.title: Servers With Nvidia Chips Were Smuggled Into China, U.S. Indictment Says
item.description: Super Micro Computer placed two employees on leave and fired a contractor after charges of diverting U.S.-assembled servers to China, violating export control laws.

item.title: China Targets Lawyers Defending Jailed Christian Leaders
item.description: The arrest of pastor Ezra Jin has drawn criticism from Secretary of State Marco Rubio and members of Congress.

item.title: U.K. Government Borrowing Rose in February
item.description: The government borrowed £14.3 billion that month, up £2.2 billion from a year earlier as interest payments hit a record high.

item.title: Rupee, Most Other Asian Currencies Weaken on Economic Growth Worries
item.description: The Indian rupee and most other Asian currencies weakened against the dollar amid worries over economic growth in the region.

item.title: Beijing’s Big Problem: An Incredible Shrinking Economy
item.description: China has taken pride in its explosive growth over the decades. But now, its economy is falling behind because of deflation and a weak currency.

item.title: In Nearly 90 Truth Social Posts, Trump Narrates the War in Iran
item.description: Twenty days into the conflict, the president has used social media to conduct his own unique form of online diplomacy.

item.title: The Critical Energy Assets Hit in the Gulf
item.description: Refineries, gas plants and other types of infrastructure have been struck, worsening an oil-and-gas crisis.

item.title: U.S. War Planes and Helicopters Kick Off Battle to Reopen Hormuz
item.description: The effort has taken on new urgency nearly three weeks into the war with Iran, as oil prices soar above $100 a barrel.

item.title: Taiwan’s All-Hands-on-Deck Plan to Fend Off China
item.description: A growing cohort of self-defense zealots is embracing a mission to get the island ready for an attack.

item.title: Opinion | How Trump Can Punish Spain
item.description: Given Madrid’s refusal to help against Iran, U.S. laws dealing with boycotts of Israel could impose economic costs.

item.title: Opinion | The Next Cuban Revolution
item.description: The regime is on the brink, but the wrong U.S. move could save it.

item.title: Opinion | The Enemy in Iran in One Lesson
item.description: The regime resumes hanging protesters, including a national team wrestler.

item.title: Opinion | California’s Climate of Fuel Insecurity
item.description: Refinery shutdowns risk emergencies at West Coast military bases.

item.title: Opinion | Will Trump Finish the Job in Iran?
item.description: The Islamic Republic has been severely degraded, but it will continue to pose a threat to the Gulf.

item.title: Trump’s Iran War Frays Ties With Allies as Oil Prices Surge
item.description: Multiple countries are offering assistance but not on the scale that the White House wants.

item.title: Stocks Decline and Brent Crude Retreats After Touching $119
item.description: U.S. stocks climbed back from earlier declines, trading down less than 0.5% on the day.

item.title: Iran War Scrambles Calculus for Central Banks
item.description: The Iran war and its impact on energy prices is forcing global central banks to reassess their interest rate plans.

item.title: Opinion | The Credit Engine Behind China’s Economy Is Sputtering
item.description: Cheap lending to low-quality borrowers renders one of Beijing’s prime policy tools less efficient.

item.title: Ukraine Suffers Money Setback After Hungary Blocks $100 Billion From Europe
item.description: European leaders approved vital cash for Kyiv last year but Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán now opposes it over anger with Kyiv.

item.title: U.S. mulls lifting oil sanctions on Iran as it hits key Gulf energy sites
item.description: The price of Brent crude briefly climbed past $117 per barrel as Qatar, Saudi Arabia and Kuwait reported more attacks on infrastructure and the U.S. and Iran traded threats.

item.title: EU Leaders Wrangle With Middle East Fallout at Brussels Summit
item.description: They have called for de-escalation of the conflict as the bloc grapples with volatile energy prices and blocked trade routes.

item.title: Shell Says Production at Pearl GTL Plant in Qatar Halted After Iranian Attack
item.description: One of the two trains at Pearl GTL, the world’s largest gas-to-liquids plant, sustained damage after Iranian missiles struck the Ras Laffan complex in Qatar.

item.title: ‘It’s a Nightmare’: Rapid Battlefield Shifts Leave Markets Trading Blind
item.description: In the fog of war, precise data is scarce and the battlefield is shifting too fast for investors, leading to gyrating oil prices.

item.title: Best High-Yield Savings Accounts for March 2026: Up to 5.00%
item.description: These are the accounts that offer the best interest rates—without burdensome fees, gimmicks or other hassles

item.title: U.K. Jobless Rate Remains Near Five-Year High
item.description: The reading comes as war in the Middle East threatens to hit growth and push the jobless rate even higher in the coming months.

item.title: China Isn’t Planning to Invade Taiwan in 2027, U.S. Concludes
item.description: Beijing would prefer to take control of the self-ruled island without resorting to force, according to the U.S. intelligence community.

item.title: Oil Nears $120 as Key Gulf Energy Hubs Come Under Attack
item.description: A fresh wave of attacks hitting some of the world’s most critical energy facilities has stoked fears of deeper and more prolonged disruptions to global supplies.

item.title: Economic fallout from U.S.-led war is hitting the rest of the world harder
item.description: Because of greater exposure to disruptions in the Middle East, such as high natural gas prices, businesses and consumers outside the U.S. are being hit harder by the war in Iran.

item.title: Escalating Attacks on Gulf Energy Assets Plunge Iran War Into New Phase
item.description: Strikes targeting infrastructure in Iran, Qatar and Saudi Arabia have put strategic oil-and-gas facilities at the center of the conflict.

item.title: China Hoped Trump Summit Would Cement Its Superpower Status. Now Xi Has to Wait.
item.description: President Trump’s postponement of the planned meeting signaled that the U.S. still sets the global agenda, and not Beijing.

item.title: The Land-Based Missiles That Are Crucial to America’s Air War on Iran
item.description: The weapons add a new dimension to the U.S. attack and raise the possibility that Gulf allies are launch points.

item.title: A U.S. Citizen Now Runs Mexico’s Top Drug Cartel—and Targeting Him Is Complicated
item.description: The California-born stepson of the late kingpin Nemesio ‘El Mencho’ Oseguera, known for his brutality, enjoys constitutional protections.

item.title: What U.S. Marines Can Do to Help Reopen the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The Marine Corps unit expected to arrive in the Middle East next week could help seize one or more of the strategic islands off Iran’s southern coast to counter Iranian attacks on shipping.

item.title: Trump’s Greenland Pursuit Gives Danish Leader New Lease on Life
item.description: Mette Frederiksen is seeking a third term leading the nation of six million people, after getting a polling boost from the president’s push for the territory.

item.title: Pakistani strike killed hundreds, Afghanistan says, as regional conflicts boil
item.description: As attention focuses on Iran, the conflict between two of its neighbors is escalating.

item.title: Israel steps up assassinations as Iran war widens to energy facilities
item.description: The conflict continues to roil global energy markets. On Wednesday, an attack on Iran’s South Pars gas field sent energy prices soaring.

item.title: Opinion | Brendan Carr and the TikTok Dodge
item.description: The FCC chief aims at networks few watch, while Trump gives the Chinese site a break.

item.title: Opinion | Tulsi Gabbard’s Resistance Shop
item.description: The Director of National Intelligence hires another Iran policy opponent.

item.title: Venezuelan Leader Ditches Defense Minister for Another Hard-Liner
item.description: The successor to Vladimir Padrino, whose forces failed to protect Nicolás Maduro from U.S. commandos, signals a lack of urgency on a democratic overhaul.

item.title: Opinion | Orbán and the ‘Green Deal’ Pollute This Hungarian City
item.description: Debrecen pays the price for the EU’s fixation on EVs.

item.title: How Lego Became a Go-To Meme of the Propaganda Wars
item.description: Throwback messaging using toys and videogames—with a dash of AI—is lowering defenses and opening new avenues for the information wars between rival states.

item.title: Opinion | Iran’s Ayatollahs Attack the Idea of the U.A.E.
item.description: They hate seeing an open, resilient economy flourish in one of the world’s most contested regions.

item.title: Iran War: Five Things to Know
item.description: The U.S.-Israeli campaign has threatened the global oil supply and has no clear end date.

item.title: Gabbard tells senators Iranian regime is degraded but still intact
item.description: The director of national intelligence provided the Senate Intelligence Committee with mixed messages about the state of Iran’s nuclear program before the war began.

item.title: Opinion | America Bombs Iran, and Zelensky Suddenly Holds Aces
item.description: ‘You don’t have the cards,’ Trump said a year ago. Today U.S. allies implore Ukraine for drone help.

item.title: Best Car Insurance Companies of 2026
item.description: Progressive, Travelers and Geico and among the top picks in our extensive analysis.

item.title: Canada’s Population Dips for Second Straight Quarter, Leading to First Annual Fall On Record
item.description: Canada’s population fell last year for the for the first time on record going back to World War II, a stark shift from the growth seen in recent years after the federal government tightened immigration policy.

item.title: What a Frozen, Abandoned Military Base Reveals About Trump’s Greenland Ambitions
item.description: Our correspondent traveled 450 miles across Greenland to see what it would take for the U.S. to reopen military bases on the island.

item.title: How China Is Quietly Helping an Isolated Iran Survive
item.description: From buying oil to selling rocket parts, these are some of the ways China is giving Iran critical support.

item.title: How China Is Quietly Helping an Isolated Iran Survive
item.description: From buying oil to selling rocket parts, these are some of the ways China is giving Iran critical support.

item.title: Rubio says Cuba’s efforts to improve its economy are failing
item.description: Cuba “can’t fix” its economy, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said, as President Donald Trump said his administration will be “doing something with Cuba very soon.”

item.title: Japan’s leader heads to D.C. bearing gifts of peace for a president at war
item.description: Japan’s super-popular prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, will visit the White House on Thursday, as President Donald Trump looks to allies for military help against Iran.

item.title: Which Iranian Leaders Have Been Killed and Who Survived?
item.description: Allies kill a large number of high-ranking Iranian officials amid fast-accumulating damage from airstrikes and a growing crop of intelligence.

item.title: Israel Is Hunting Down Iranian Regime Members in Their Hideouts, One by One
item.description: The killings of top officials mark milestones in a fierce campaign to bring down the Tehran government.

item.title: Israel Is Hunting Down Iranian Regime Members in Their Hideouts, One by One
item.description: The killings of top officials mark milestones in a fierce campaign to bring down the Tehran government.

item.title: This Emirati billionaire put a voice to Gulf anger over Trump’s war in Iran
item.description: In a post on X, the hotel magnate lambasted the president for not considering collateral damage, although he later told The Post: “I blame Trump, but I blame the Iranians more.”

item.title: Hezbollah ignites blowback from its base as over 1 million flee war with Israel
item.description: The militant group’s attacks on Israel have sparked anger even among its most loyal Shiite supporters in Lebanon, weakening its clout as the war widens.

item.title: In Delaying China Summit, Trump Faces a Familiar Mideast Distraction
item.description: The war in Iran is the latest example of why several presidents have found it so hard to focus on the U.S.’s most formidable rival.

item.title: Asia Equities Gain Ahead of Fed, Oil Retreats But Stays High
item.description: Asian equity markets advanced broadly on Wednesday after a positive lead from Wall Street overnight, while oil retreated but stayed high as the conflict in the Middle East continued.

item.title: World Baseball Classic Win Over U.S. Finally Gives Venezuelans Something to Cheer
item.description: Venezuelans at home and abroad celebrate a victory over a heavily favored U.S. team as a moment of national pride.

item.title: A Visual Guide to the Scale of the U.S.-Israeli Air War on Iran
item.description: The allies have killed Islamic Republic leaders and destroyed thousands of military targets in an attempt to bring down Tehran’s regime.

item.title: Israel says it killed Iran’s top security official, along with paramilitary boss
item.description: Ali Larijani, Iran’s lead security official, and Basij commander Gholamreza Soleimani were “eliminated,” Israel said. Iran has not commented on the strikes.

item.title: Nvidia Says It Is Restarting Production of AI Chips for Sale in China
item.description: CEO Jensen Huang said the chip maker’s supply chain is “fired up” after months of mixed signals from the Chinese market.

item.title: European leaders rebuff Trump’s call to open the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Trump has expressed frustration over a lack of military assistance from allies, but European leaders are reluctant to join a conflict he started without consulting them.

item.title: U.S. tells all its embassies to ‘immediately’ review security after strikes
item.description: The State Department order to review security at “ALL posts worldwide” follows persistent attacks on U.S. embassies, with almost 300 reported in Iraq alone.

item.title: Senior official openly breaks with White House, resigns over war
item.description: Joe Kent, a close aide to Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard, cited deliberate Israeli “misinformation” and lies to President Donald Trump about a “swift path to victory.”

item.title: Russia Is Sharing Satellite Imagery and Drone Technology With Iran
item.description: Moscow has expanded intelligence sharing and military cooperation to help keep Tehran in the fight against U.S. and Israeli military might.

item.title: Cuba Struggles to Revive Obsolete Power Grid After Nationwide Blackout
item.description: Residents endure long electricity outages as the island’s economy grinds to a halt, fueling unrest.

item.title: America Gave Up Some of Its Last Minesweepers. Then Iran Made Them Necessary Again.
item.description: The Strait of Hormuz could prove to be a testing ground for unmanned technology and AI in mine clearance.

item.title: Pakistan Airstrikes on Kabul Hit Drug Rehab Clinic, Killing Civilians
item.description: Afghanistan’s Taliban government said hundreds were killed at the treatment clinic; Pakistan denies that it hit a civilian target.

item.title: Trump Shifts U.S.-China Strategy on Trade to Dealmaking
item.description: Plus, President Trump has asked Beijing to delay his summit with Xi and some have signaled renewed openness to bilateral investment.

item.title: Takeaways From WSJ Reporting on the CIA’s Dealings With an Ex-Chevron Executive
item.description: The executive’s involvement offers a window into how Trump has embraced the energy industry’s playbook for dealing with autocrats.

item.title: China’s Economy Off to Steady Start in 2026 Amid Lowered Expectations
item.description: A better-than-expected performance in the first two months of the year opens space for Beijing to pursue its goal of shifting toward consumption-led growth.

item.title: He Was Chevron’s Man in Venezuela—and a CIA Informant
item.description: After retiring from Chevron, Ali Moshiri warned the Trump administration it would face a morass if it tried to install the democratic opposition.

item.title: China Resumes Military Flights Around Taiwan After Sudden 10-Day Hiatus
item.description: Beijing hasn’t explained why it abruptly stopped what had become a routine practice, nor its resumption.

item.title: For Xi, Iran War Reinforces View of U.S. as Dangerous Superpower
item.description: Trump’s assaults on Iran and Venezuela—both Chinese allies and key suppliers of oil for its energy-hungry economy—could reaffirm Beijing’s mistrust of Washington.

item.title: Russia Shuts Off Internet in Moscow as It Tests Nationwide Censorship System
item.description: Mobile internet access has been shut off in Moscow in recent days after similar outages in dozens of regions.

item.title: A Visual Guide to How Iran Is Holding the Strait of Hormuz Hostage
item.description: Ships in the Persian Gulf have been sitting ducks for Tehran.

item.title: U.K. Economy Unexpectedly Stalled in January
item.description: A sustained return to modest growth is likely to be hindered by the rise in energy prices and uncertainty that has accompanied the Middle East conflict.

item.title: Xi Enforces His Demand for Ethnic Unity Across China
item.description: After crackdowns on Uyghurs and Tibetans, a new law threatens punishment for anyone who doesn’t embrace the common language and culture.

item.title: A Punch in the Face, a Barefoot General’s Fight and the Coup That Unraveled
item.description: A scramble for power in West Africa kicked off 24 hours of gunbattles, betrayal, kidnapping and superstition.

item.title: How To Refinance a Car Loan
item.description: Navigate the car loan refinancing process to potentially lower your interest rate and monthly payments.

item.title: Russia Lures Recruits From Africa to Feed Its Ukraine War Machine
item.description: As the fighting in Ukraine drags on, Moscow is casting its net farther afield to find fresh reinforcements.

item.title: Harry, William and the Royal Rift That Won’t Heal
item.description: A schism in the House of Windsor looks irreconcilable. Inside the rift between Prince Harry and Prince William that could linger for decades to come.

item.title: As Iran War Pushes Up Oil Prices, Putin Can Barely Conceal a Smirk
item.description: The Trump administration has rolled back some efforts to put pressure on Russia’s economy, and held out the possibility of easing sanctions further.

item.title: Troops Mass as Landlocked Ethiopia Chases Access to the Sea
item.description: Decades after it lost its coast to Eritrea, the country has moved forces close to the border region—raising fears of another civil war with nearby rebels.

item.title: Russia Secretly Sharing Location of U.S. Targets With Iran, U.S. Officials Say
item.description: In a sign of deepening ties, satellite data from Moscow could help Tehran hit American forces around the Middle East.

item.title: King Charles Has Renounced His Brother. Is It Enough to Save His Own Approval Rating?
item.description: The investigation into Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor comes at a shaky moment for the British royal family, and for the king himself.

item.title: They Went to Dubai for Sun and Low Taxes. They Wound Up in a War Zone.
item.description: The Gulf city has emerged as a premier destination for expats. Now that it is caught in the Mideast conflict, some of them are finding little sympathy back home.

item.title: Canada, India Strike $1.9 Billion Uranium Supply Agreement as They Firm Ties
item.description: Canada has signed a string of agreements with India aimed at deepening ties in areas like critical minerals and energy as it seeks more alliances amid tensions with the U.S.

item.title: Marriott International Wins Court Battle Over Maasai Mara Safari Camp in Kenya
item.description: A Kenyan court dismissed a lawsuit by a Maasai leader and environmentalist who alleged a Ritz-Carlton luxury camp blocked a route of the famous Serengeti migration.

item.title: As Trump’s African Peace Deal Teetered, a Call From Rwanda Halted U.S. Sanctions
item.description: The White House says Rwanda has violated a U.S.-brokered agreement on Congo, but sanctions are on ice.

item.title: Spurned by Trump, Canada’s Carney Is Seeking New Friends Everywhere
item.description: The prime minister is traveling to India, until now a bitter rival, to mend fences and build new alliances with the world’s middle powers.

item.title: Canada’s Carney Heads to India to Deepen Commercial Ties With Indo-Pacific Region
item.description: Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney aims to accelerate efforts to deepen commercial ties with India after years of diplomatic distress.

item.title: As War Enters Fifth Year, Ukraine Shows Russian Victory Is Anything but Inevitable
item.description: Small Ukrainian counterattacks are demonstrating that Kyiv’s forces have got plenty of fight left.

item.title: U.S. Immigration Deportees Held in Secret African Detention Facility
item.description: Sending migrants to Cameroon skirted judicial orders, lawyers said.

item.title: U.S. Builds Tech Bloc to Counter China, Adds India as Newest Member
item.description: With Pax Silica, the Trump administration is retreating from an adversarial stance it took toward allies and friends last year.

item.title: Bill Gates Cancels India AI Summit Speech as Epstein Scrutiny Grows
item.description: The Microsoft co-founder canceled his keynote address as he faces growing scrutiny over his ties to the convicted sex offender.

item.title: The U.S. Navy’s New Insurance Policy for War With China Is an Australian Base
item.description: The Pentagon plans to deploy submarines to an expanding facility in allies’ strategy to deter Beijing.

item.title: Several People Missing After Landslide Buries Campground in New Zealand
item.description: Debris engulfed the site at Mount Maunganui following record rainfall. A government minister said young people were among the missing.

item.title: Can a U.S.-Greenland Partnership Work? Just Look at These Pacific Islands
item.description: U.S. agreements with three island nations have benefited both sides but have also had challenges.

item.title: Bondi Beach Terror Suspects Condemned ‘Zionists’ and Amassed an Arsenal
item.description: Court documents reveal what police say were the movements and motivation of Sajid and Naveed Akram.

item.title: Bondi Beach Shooting Suspect Charged With Terrorism, Murder
item.description: Police charged 24-year-old Naveed Akram after he woke up from a coma.
